metadata.title: Monkey: Optimal Navigable Key-Value Store
metadata.titleTranslation: Monkey：最优的可导航键值存储

sections.title: Abstract
sections.originalMarkdown:
In this paper, we show that key-value stores backed by an LSM-tree exhibit an intrinsic trade-off between lookup cost, update cost, and main memory footprint, yet all existing designs expose a suboptimal and difficult to tune trade-off among these metrics. We pinpoint the problem to the fact that all modern key-value stores suboptimally co-tune the merge policy, the buffer size, and the Bloom filters’ false positive rates in each level.

We present Monkey, an LSM-based key-value store that strikes the optimal balance between the costs of updates and lookups with any given main memory budget. The insight is that worst-case lookup cost is proportional to the sum of the false positive rates of the Bloom filters across all levels of the LSM-tree. Contrary to state-of-the-art key-value stores that assign a fixed number of bits-per-element to all Bloom filters, Monkey allocates memory to filters across different levels so as to minimize this sum. We show analytically that Monkey reduces the asymptotic complexity of the worst-case lookup I/O cost, and we verify empirically using an implementation on top of LevelDB that Monkey reduces lookup latency by an increasing margin as the data volume grows (50% − 80% for the data sizes we experimented with). Furthermore, we map the LSM-tree design space onto a closed-form model that enables co-tuning the merge policy, the buffer size and the filters’ false positive rates to trade among lookup cost, update cost and/or main memory, depending on the workload (proportion of lookups and updates), the dataset (number and size of entries), and the underlying hardware (main memory available, disk vs. flash). We show how to use this model to answer what-if design questions about how changes in environmental parameters impact performance and how to adapt the various LSM-tree design elements accordingly.
sections.translationMarkdown:
本文表明，基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储在查询成本、更新成本和主存占用之间存在内在权衡，但所有现有设计在这些指标之间提供的权衡都是次优且难以调优的。我们将问题归因于：所有现代键值存储都以次优方式联合调节合并策略、缓冲区大小以及各层 Bloom filter 的假阳性率。

我们提出 Monkey，这是一种基于 LSM 的键值存储，能够在任意给定主存预算下，在更新成本和查询成本之间取得最优平衡。其关键洞见是，最坏情况下的查询成本与 LSM-tree 所有层的 Bloom filter 假阳性率之和成正比。与为所有 Bloom filter 分配固定每元素位数的先进键值存储不同，Monkey 在不同层的过滤器之间分配内存，以使该总和最小。我们从分析上证明，Monkey 降低了最坏情况查询 I/O 成本的渐近复杂度；并通过一个构建在 LevelDB 之上的实现进行实证验证：随着数据量增大，Monkey 降低查询延迟的幅度也不断增大（在我们实验的数据规模下为 50% − 80%）。此外，我们将 LSM-tree 设计空间映射为一个闭式模型，使系统能够联合调节合并策略、缓冲区大小和过滤器假阳性率，从而根据工作负载（查询和更新的比例）、数据集（条目数量和大小）以及底层硬件（可用主存、磁盘还是闪存），在查询成本、更新成本和/或主存之间进行权衡。我们还展示了如何使用该模型回答假设性设计问题，即环境参数的变化会如何影响性能，以及应如何相应调整 LSM-tree 的各种设计要素。

sections.title: 1. INTRODUCTION
sections.originalMarkdown:
**LSM-Based Key-Value Stores.** Modern key-value stores that maintain application data persistently typically use a Log-Structured-Merge-tree (LSM-tree) [27] as their storage layer. In contrast to traditional storage paradigms that involve in-place updates to persistent storage, LSM-trees perform out-of-place updates thereby enabling (1) high throughput for updates [29] and (2) good space-efficiency as data is stored compactly [14] (as they do not need to keep free space at every node to allow in-place updates). They do so by buffering all updates in main memory, flushing the buffer to disk as a sorted run whenever it fills up, and organizing disk-resident runs into a number of levels of increasing sizes. To bound the number of runs that a lookup probes to find a target key, runs of similar sizes (i.e., at the same level) are sort-merged and pushed to the next deeper level when the current one becomes full. To speed up point lookups, which are common in practice [6], every run has an associated Bloom filter in main memory that probabilistically allows to skip a run if it does not contain the target key. In addition, every run has a set of fence pointers in main memory that map values to disk pages of the run (effectively maintaining min-max information for each page of a run) and thereby allow searching a run for a target key in a single I/O. This design is adopted in a wide number of modern key-value stores including LevelDB [19] and BigTable [12] at Google, RocksDB [15] at Facebook, Cassandra [21], HBase [5] and Accumulo [3] at Apache, Voldemort [25] at LinkedIn, Dynamo [13] at Amazon, WiredTiger [34] at MongoDB, and bLSM [29] and cLSM [18] at Yahoo. Various relational stores today also support this design. For example, MySQL can run on RocksDB using MyRocks [14], which prefixes keys to indicate which table they belong to and maps from SQL commands to RocksDB commands. Moreover, SQLite recently changed its storage engine from a B-tree in SQLite3 to an LSM-tree in SQLite4, meaning that every table is now an LSM-tree [31].

*Figure 1: State-of-the-art LSM-tree based key-value stores are not tuned along the Pareto curve. As a result, they are unable to maximize throughput. In contrast, Monkey is tuned along the Paretro Frontier, and it can navigate it to find the best trade-off for a given application to maximize throughput.*

**The Problem: Suboptimal Design Tuning.** In this paper, we closely investigate the design space of LSM-trees and show that LSM-based key-value stores exhibit an intrinsic trade-off among lookup cost, update cost, and main memory footprint (as discussed for various data structures in [8, 7]). Existing systems strike a suboptimal trade-off among these metrics. Figure 1 shows this graphically using cost models (described later) and the default configuration settings for several state-of-the-art systems as found in their latest source code and documentation. Existing systems are not tuned along the optimal Pareto curve beyond which it is impossible to improve lookup cost without harming update cost and vice versa with a given main memory budget. As a result, they cannot maximize throughput for a given main memory budget and application workload.

*We pinpoint the problem to the fact that all LSM-based key-value stores suboptimally co-tune the core design choices in LSM-trees: the merge policy, the size ratio between levels, the buffer size, and the Bloom filters’ false positive rates.*

The first problem is that existing designs assign the same false positive rate (i.e., number of bits per element) to every Bloom filter regardless of the size of the run that it corresponds to. Our insight is that the worst-case point lookup cost over the whole LSM-tree is proportional to the sum of the false positive rates of all filters. Assigning equal false positive rates to all filters, however, does not minimize this sum. The reason is that maintaining the same false positive rate across all runs means that larger runs have proportionally larger Bloom filters, whereas the I/O cost of probing any run is the same regardless of its size (due to each run having fence pointers in main memory that allow direct access to the relevant disk page). As a result, the runs at the largest level take up the majority of the overall memory budget allocated to the Bloom filters without yielding a significant reduction in lookup cost.

The second problem is that the relationship between the different design knobs and performance is non-linear, and so it is difficult to co-tune the various design options in the LSM-tree design space to optimize performance. For example, the available main memory budget cannot only be used to increase Bloom filter accuracy but it can also be used to increase the size of the LSM-tree buffer (which implies better lookup performance as there are fewer levels to probe and better update performance as there are fewer levels to merge entries through). However, it is unclear how to allocate a fixed main memory budget among these structures to strike the best balance. In addition, it is possible to trade between lookup cost and update cost by varying the size ratio between levels and by switching the merge policy between leveling and tiering, which support one run per level vs. multiple runs per level respectively. Again, however, it is difficult to predict how a given change would impact performance given the complexity of the design space.

**The Solution: Monkey.** In this paper, we map the design space of LSM-trees. This allows us (1) to holistically tune critical design knobs thereby achieving performance along the Pareto curve (as shown in Figure 1), and (2) to accurately navigate the Pareto curve to find the best trade-off for a given application.

We introduce Monkey: Optimal Navigable Key-Value Store. Monkey reaches the Pareto curve by using a novel analytical solution that minimizes lookup cost by allocating main memory among the Bloom filters so as to minimize the sum of their false positive rates. The core idea is setting the false positive rate of each Bloom filter to be proportional to the number of entries in the run that it corresponds to (meaning that the false positive rates for shallower levels are exponentially decreasing). The intuition is that any given amount of main memory allocated to Bloom filters of larger runs brings only a relatively minor benefit in terms of how much it can decrease their false positive rates (to save I/Os). On the contrary, the same amount of memory can have a higher impact in reducing the false positive rate for smaller runs. We show analytically that this way shaves a factor of $O(L)$ from the worst-case lookup cost, where $L$ is the number of LSM-tree levels. The intuition is that the false positive rates across the levels form a geometric series, and its sum converges to a constant that is independent of $L$. The number of levels is $O(\log(\frac{N \cdot E}{M_{buffer}}))$, where $N$ is the number of entries, $E$ is the size of entries, and $M_{buffer}$ is the size of the buffer. This has two important benefits: (1) lookup cost scales better with the number and size of entries, and (2) lookup cost is independent of the buffer size thereby removing the contention in how to allocate main memory between the filters and the buffer. Therefore, Monkey scales better and is easier to tune.

The second key point in Monkey is navigating the Pareto curve to find the optimal balance between lookup cost and update cost under a given main memory budget and application workload (lookup over update ratio). To do so, we map the design space and environmental parameters that affect this balance, and we capture the worst-case cost of lookups and updates as concise closed-form expressions. While all existing major key-value store implementations expose numerous tuning knobs, Monkey is novel in that it allows precise navigation of the design space with predictable results, making it easy to set-up or adapt a key-value store installation.

**Contributions.** In summary, our contributions are as follows.

- In Section 3, we show that key-value stores backed by an LSM-tree exhibit a navigable trade-off among lookup cost, update cost, and main memory footprint; yet state-of-the-art key-value stores are not tuned along the optimal trade-off curve because they do not allocate main memory optimally among the Bloom filters and the LSM-tree’s buffer.

- In Section 4, we introduce Monkey, an LSM-tree based key-value store that optimizes lookup cost by allocating main memory among the Bloom filters so as to minimize the sum of their false positive rates. We show analytically that Monkey’s Bloom filter tuning (1) reduces the asymptotic complexity of lookup cost thereby scaling better for large datasets, and (2) removes the dependence of lookup cost on the LSM-tree’s buffer size thereby simplifying system tuning.

- We identify the tuning and environment parameters that determine worst-case performance and model worst-case lookup and update costs as closed-form expressions. We show how to use these models to find the holistic tuning that (1) maximizes throughput under a uniformly random workload with any lookup/update ratio, and (2) maximize the lower-bound on throughput for any other workload.

- We also show how to use the model to answer what-if design and environmental questions. For instance, if we change (i) the main memory budget, (ii) the proportion of reads and writes in the workload, (iii) the number and/or size of data entries, or (iv) the underlying storage medium (e.g., flash vs. disk), how should we adapt the LSM-tree design, and what is the impact on performance?

- In Section 5, we evaluate Monkey using an implementation on top of LevelDB by applying a wide range of application lookup patterns (i.e., targeting existing vs non-existing keys and varying temporal localities). Monkey improves lookup latency by 50% − 80% in these experiments.

**Online Demo.** To provide further understanding of the impact of Monkey we provide an online interactive demo[^1].

[^1]: http://daslab.seas.harvard.edu/monkey/
sections.translationMarkdown:
**基于 LSM 的键值存储。** 持久保存应用数据的现代键值存储通常使用日志结构合并树（Log-Structured-Merge-tree，LSM-tree）[27] 作为其存储层。传统存储范式会对持久存储进行原地更新；与之不同，LSM-tree 执行异地更新，因此能够实现：(1) 较高的更新吞吐量 [29]；(2) 良好的空间效率，因为数据以紧凑形式存储 [14]（无需在每个节点预留空闲空间来支持原地更新）。具体做法是：将所有更新缓存在主存中；每当缓冲区写满时，将其作为一个有序 run 刷写到磁盘；并把驻留在磁盘上的 run 组织成若干大小逐层递增的层级。为了限制一次查询在寻找目标键时必须探查的 run 数量，大小相近（即位于同一层）的 run 会进行排序合并，并在当前层写满时被推入下一更深层。为了加速实践中常见的点查询 [6]，每个 run 都在主存中配有一个 Bloom filter；如果目标键不在该 run 中，该过滤器能够以概率方式让查询跳过该 run。此外，每个 run 还在主存中维护一组 fence pointer，将键值映射到该 run 的磁盘页（实际上为 run 的每个页面维护最小值—最大值信息），从而只需一次 I/O 即可在一个 run 中搜索目标键。许多现代键值存储都采用了这种设计，包括 Google 的 LevelDB [19] 和 BigTable [12]、Facebook 的 RocksDB [15]、Apache 的 Cassandra [21]、HBase [5] 和 Accumulo [3]、LinkedIn 的 Voldemort [25]、Amazon 的 Dynamo [13]、MongoDB 的 WiredTiger [34]，以及 Yahoo 的 bLSM [29] 和 cLSM [18]。如今，各类关系型存储也支持这种设计。例如，MySQL 可以通过 MyRocks [14] 运行在 RocksDB 上；MyRocks 为键添加前缀以指示其所属表，并把 SQL 命令映射为 RocksDB 命令。此外，SQLite 最近将其存储引擎从 SQLite3 中的 B-tree 改为了 SQLite4 中的 LSM-tree，这意味着现在每张表都是一棵 LSM-tree [31]。

*图 1：先进的基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储并未沿 Pareto 曲线进行调优，因此无法最大化吞吐量。相比之下，Monkey 沿 Pareto 前沿进行调优，并能在该前沿上移动，为给定应用找到能够最大化吞吐量的最佳权衡。*

**问题：次优的设计调优。** 本文深入研究 LSM-tree 的设计空间，并表明基于 LSM 的键值存储在查询成本、更新成本和主存占用之间存在内在权衡（各种数据结构中的此类权衡见 [8, 7]）。现有系统在这些指标之间取得的是次优权衡。图 1 使用后文介绍的成本模型，并结合多个先进系统最新源代码和文档中的默认配置，以图形方式展示了这一点。现有系统并未沿最优 Pareto 曲线进行调优；在给定主存预算下，一旦超过这条曲线，就不可能在不损害更新成本的前提下改善查询成本，反之亦然。因此，它们无法在给定主存预算和应用工作负载下实现最大吞吐量。

*我们将问题归因于：所有基于 LSM 的键值存储都以次优方式联合调节 LSM-tree 中的核心设计选择，即合并策略、层间大小比、缓冲区大小以及 Bloom filter 的假阳性率。*

第一个问题是，无论 Bloom filter 所对应 run 的大小如何，现有设计都为每个 Bloom filter 分配相同的假阳性率（即相同的每元素位数）。我们的洞见是，整个 LSM-tree 上最坏情况下的点查询成本与所有过滤器假阳性率之和成正比。然而，为所有过滤器分配相同的假阳性率并不能使这个总和最小。原因在于，在所有 run 上保持相同的假阳性率，意味着较大的 run 具有按比例更大的 Bloom filter；而无论 run 大小如何，探查任意 run 的 I/O 成本都相同（因为每个 run 都在主存中维护 fence pointer，可直接访问相关磁盘页）。因此，最大层上的 run 占用了分配给 Bloom filter 的大部分总内存预算，却不能显著降低查询成本。

第二个问题是，不同设计旋钮与性能之间的关系是非线性的，因此很难联合调节 LSM-tree 设计空间中的各种设计选项来优化性能。例如，可用主存预算不仅可以用于提高 Bloom filter 的准确率，也可用于增大 LSM-tree 缓冲区（层数更少、需要探查的层也更少，因而查询性能更好；条目需要合并经过的层更少，因而更新性能也更好）。然而，如何在这些结构之间分配固定的主存预算以取得最佳平衡并不明确。此外，还可以通过改变层间大小比，以及在 leveling 和 tiering 两种合并策略之间切换，在查询成本与更新成本之间进行权衡；这两种策略分别允许每层存在一个 run 和多个 run。但由于设计空间十分复杂，仍然很难预测某项给定变更会如何影响性能。

**解决方案：Monkey。** 本文对 LSM-tree 的设计空间进行了映射。这使我们能够：(1) 整体调节关键设计旋钮，从而取得位于 Pareto 曲线上的性能（如图 1 所示）；(2) 准确地沿 Pareto 曲线移动，为给定应用找到最佳权衡。

我们提出 Monkey：最优且可导航的键值存储。Monkey 采用一种新的分析解，在各 Bloom filter 之间分配主存，以最小化其假阳性率之和，进而最小化查询成本，从而到达 Pareto 曲线。核心思想是，让每个 Bloom filter 的假阳性率与其所对应 run 中的条目数成正比（这意味着越浅层的假阳性率呈指数下降）。直观上，将任意给定数量的主存分配给较大 run 的 Bloom filter，只能略微降低其假阳性率，因而在节省 I/O 方面收益较小。相反，同样数量的内存可以更显著地降低较小 run 的假阳性率。我们从分析上证明，这种方法能从最坏情况查询成本中削去一个 $O(L)$ 因子，其中 $L$ 为 LSM-tree 的层数。其直觉是，各层的假阳性率构成一个几何级数，其和收敛到一个与 $L$ 无关的常数。层数为 $O(\log(\frac{N \cdot E}{M_{buffer}}))$，其中 $N$ 是条目数量，$E$ 是条目大小，$M_{buffer}$ 是缓冲区大小。这带来两项重要优势：(1) 查询成本随条目数量和大小增长时具有更好的可扩展性；(2) 查询成本与缓冲区大小无关，从而消除了在过滤器和缓冲区之间分配主存时的冲突。因此，Monkey 的可扩展性更好，也更容易调优。

Monkey 的第二个关键点，是沿 Pareto 曲线移动，以便在给定主存预算和应用工作负载（查询与更新的比例）下，找到查询成本与更新成本之间的最优平衡。为此，我们映射了影响这一平衡的设计空间和环境参数，并将查询与更新的最坏情况成本表示为简洁的闭式表达式。虽然所有现有主流键值存储实现都暴露了大量调优旋钮，但 Monkey 的新颖之处在于，它能够以可预测的结果精确地在设计空间中导航，因而可以轻松建立或调整一个键值存储部署。

**贡献。** 概括而言，我们的贡献如下。

- 在第 3 节中，我们证明基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储在查询成本、更新成本和主存占用之间存在可导航的权衡；然而，先进的键值存储并未沿最优权衡曲线进行调优，因为它们没有在 Bloom filter 和 LSM-tree 缓冲区之间以最优方式分配主存。

- 在第 4 节中，我们提出 Monkey，这是一种基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储，它通过在 Bloom filter 之间分配主存，使其假阳性率之和最小，从而优化查询成本。我们从分析上证明，Monkey 的 Bloom filter 调优：(1) 降低了查询成本的渐近复杂度，因此面对大型数据集时具有更好的可扩展性；(2) 消除了查询成本对 LSM-tree 缓冲区大小的依赖，从而简化系统调优。

- 我们识别出决定最坏情况性能的调优参数和环境参数，并将最坏情况下的查询和更新成本建模为闭式表达式。我们展示了如何使用这些模型寻找整体调优方案，使其：(1) 在具有任意查询/更新比例的均匀随机工作负载下最大化吞吐量；(2) 对任何其他工作负载最大化吞吐量的下界。

- 我们还展示了如何使用该模型回答关于设计和环境的假设性问题。例如，如果改变：(i) 主存预算；(ii) 工作负载中的读写比例；(iii) 数据条目的数量和/或大小；或 (iv) 底层存储介质（例如闪存与磁盘），应如何调整 LSM-tree 的设计，性能又会受到什么影响？

- 在第 5 节中，我们使用构建在 LevelDB 之上的实现评估 Monkey，并采用了广泛的应用查询模式（即分别以存在和不存在的键为目标，并改变时间局部性）。在这些实验中，Monkey 将查询延迟降低了 50% − 80%。

**在线演示。** 为帮助进一步理解 Monkey 的影响，我们提供了一个在线交互式演示[^1]。

[^1]: http://daslab.seas.harvard.edu/monkey/

sections.title: 2. BACKGROUND
sections.originalMarkdown:
This section provides the necessary background on LSM-trees. An LSM-tree stores key-value pairs. A key identifies an object of the application and allows retrieving its value. For the discussion in this paper both keys and values are stored inside the LSM-tree, although it is also possible to store large values outside of the LSM-tree and point to them using fixed-width pointers attached to keys [26]. For ease of presentation, all figures in this paper show only keys but can be thought of as key-value pairs.

**Buffering Updates.** Figure 2 illustrates an LSM-tree and a list of terms used throughout the paper. An LSM-tree consists conceptually of $L$ levels. Level 0 refers to an in-memory buffer, and the rest of the levels refer to data in secondary storage. An LSM-tree optimizes for inserts, updates, and deletes (henceforth just referred to as updates) by immediately storing them in the buffer at Level 0 without having to access secondary storage (there is a flag attached to each entry to indicate if it is a delete). If an update refers to a key which already exists in the buffer then the original entry is replaced in-place and only the latest one survives.

When the buffer’s capacity is reached, its entries are sorted by key into an array and flushed to Level 1 in secondary storage. We refer to such arrays as runs. We denote the number of bits of main memory allocated to the buffer as $M_{buffer}$, and we define it as $M_{buffer} = P \cdot B \cdot E$, where $B$ is the number of entries that fit into a disk page, $P$ is the amount of main memory in terms of disk pages allocated to the buffer, and $E$ is the average size of data entries. For example, in LevelDB the default buffer size is 2 MB.

The runs at Level 1 and higher are immutable. Each Level $i$ has a capacity threshold of $B \cdot P \cdot T^i$ entries, where $T$ is a tuning parameter denoting the size ratio between the capacities of adjacent levels. Thus, levels have exponentially increasing capacities by a factor of $T$. The overall number of levels is given by Equation 1.

$$L = \left\lceil \log_T\left(\frac{N \cdot E}{M_{buffer}} \cdot \frac{T-1}{T}\right) \right\rceil \tag{1}$$

The size ratio $T$ has a limiting value of $T_{lim}$, where $T_{lim} = \frac{N \cdot E}{M_{buffer}}$. The value of $T$ can be set anywhere between 2 and $T_{lim}$. As $T$ approaches $T_{lim}$, the number of levels $L$ approaches 1.

**Merge Operations.** To bound the number of runs that a lookup has to probe, an LSM-tree organizes runs among the different levels based on their sizes, and it merges runs of similar sizes (i.e., at the same level). There are two possible merge policies: leveling and tiering [20]. The former optimizes more for lookups and the latter more for updates. With leveling, there is at most one run per Level $i$, and any run that is moved from Level $i - 1$ to Level $i$ is immediately sort-merged with the run at Level $i$, if one exists. With tiering, up to $T$ runs can accumulate at Level $i$, at which point these runs are sort-merged. The essential difference is that a leveled LSM-tree merges runs more greedily and therefore gives a tighter bound on the overall number of runs that a lookup has to probe, but this comes at the expense of a higher amortized update cost. Figure 3 compares the behavior of merge operations for tiering and leveling when the size ratio $T$ is set to 3.

*Figure 3: Before and after a recursive merge with tiered and leveled LSM-trees where the size ratio $T$ is set to 3, and $B \cdot P$, the number of entries that fit into the buffer, is set to 2.*

If multiple runs that are being sort-merged contain entries with the same key, only the entry from the most recently-created (youngest) run is kept because it is the most up-to-date. Thus, the resulting run may be smaller than the cumulative sizes of the original runs. When a merge operation is finished, the resulting run moves to Level $i + 1$ if Level $i$ is at capacity.

**Lookups.** A point lookup starts from the buffer and traverses the levels from lowest to highest (and the runs within those levels from youngest to oldest in the case of tiering). When it finds the first matching entry it terminates. There is no need to look further because entries with the same key at older runs are superseded. A zero-result lookup (i.e., where the target key does not exist) incurs a potentially high I/O cost because it probes all runs within all levels. In contrast, a range lookup requires sort-merging all runs with an overlapping key range to identify and ignore superseded entries.

**Probing a Run.** In the original LSM-tree design from 1996 [27], each run is structured as a compact B-tree. Over the past two decades, however, main memory has become cheaper, so modern designs simply store an array of fence pointers in main memory with min/max information for every disk page of every run [15, 19]. Maintaining a flat array structure is much simpler and leads to good search performance in memory (binary search as each run is sorted). Given that the LSM-tree is on disk, these in-memory binary searches are not in the critical path of performance (I/O is). Thus, a lookup initially searches the fence pointers. If it is a point lookup, it then reads the appropriate disk page with one I/O, or if it is a range lookup it begins a scan from this page. The size of the fence pointers is modest. For example, with 16 KB disk pages and 4 byte pointers, the fence pointers are smaller by $\approx 4$ orders of magnitude than the raw data size. Stated formally, we denote the amount of main memory occupied by the fence pointers as $M_{pointers}$, and we assume throughout this work that $M_{pointers}$ is $O(\frac{N}{B})$ thereby guaranteeing that probing a run takes $O(1)$ disk I/O for point lookups.

**Bloom Filters.** To speed up point queries, every run has a corresponding Bloom filter [10] in main memory. A point lookup probes a run’s filter before accessing the run in secondary storage. If the filter returns negative, the target key does not exist in the run, and so the lookup skips accessing the run and saves one I/O. If a filter returns positive, then the target key may exist in the run, so the lookup probes the run at a cost of one I/O. If the run actually contains the key, the lookup terminates. Otherwise, we have a “false positive” and the lookup continues to probe the next run. False positives increase the I/O cost of lookups. The false positive rate (FPR) depends on (1) the number of entries in a run, and (2) the number of bits in main memory allocated to the run’s filter. This relationship is captured by the following equation [32][^2].

$$FPR = e^{-\frac{bits}{entries} \cdot \ln(2)^2} \tag{2}$$

To the best of our knowledge, all LSM-tree based key-value stores use the same number of bits-per-entry across all Bloom filters. This means that a lookup probes on average $O(e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ of the runs, where $M_{filters}$ is the overall amount of main memory allocated to the filters. As $M_{filters}$ approaches 0 or infinity, the term $O(e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ approaches 1 or 0 respectively. All implementations that we know of use 10 bits per entry for their Bloom filters by default [3, 4, 5, 15, 19, 29]. The corresponding false positive rate is $\approx 1\%$. With this tuning, an average entry size of 128 bytes (typical in practice [2]) entails the Bloom filters being $\approx 2$ orders of magnitude smaller than the raw data size.

**Cost Analysis.** We now analyze the worst-case I/O cost complexity of updates and lookups for both a tiered and a leveled LSM-tree. First, we explain how we measure these costs. For updates, we measure the *amortized worst-case I/O cost*, which accounts for the merge operations that an entry participates in after it is updated. For lookups, measure the *zero-result average worst-case I/O cost*, which is the expected number of I/Os performed by a lookup to a key that does not exist in the LSM-tree. We focus on zero-result lookups because (1) they are very common in practice [11, 29] (e.g., insert-if-not-exist queries [29]), and (2) they incur the maximum pure I/O overhead (i.e., read I/Os that do not find relevant entries to a lookup). For the rest of the paper, our use of the terms worst-case lookups and updates follows these definitions unless otherwise specified. We later also model the worst-case costs of non-zero-result lookups and range lookups for completeness.

For a tiered LSM-tree, the worst-case lookup cost is given by $O(L \cdot T \cdot e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ I/Os, because there are $O(L)$ levels, $O(T)$ runs per level, the cost of probing each run is one I/O due to the fence pointers, and we probe on average only $O(e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ of the runs. The worst-case update cost is $O(\frac{L}{B})$ I/Os, because each entry participates in $O(L)$ merge operations, i.e., one per level, and the I/O cost of copying one entry during a merge operation is $O(\frac{1}{B})$, since each write I/O copies $O(B)$ entries into the new run.

For a leveled LSM-tree, the worst-case lookup cost is given by $O(L \cdot e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ I/Os, because there are $O(L)$ levels, there is one run per level, the cost of probing a run is one I/O due to the fence pointers, and we probe $O(e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ of the runs on average. The worst-case update cost is $O(\frac{T \cdot L}{B})$ because each update is copied $O(T)$ times per level and through $O(L)$ levels overall.

[^2]: Equation 2 assumes a Bloom filter that uses the optimal number of hash functions ($\frac{bits}{entries} \ln(2)$) that minimizes the false positive rate.
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节介绍理解 LSM-tree 所需的背景知识。LSM-tree 存储键值对。键标识应用中的一个对象，并允许取回它的值。在本文的讨论中，键和值都存储在 LSM-tree 内部；不过，也可以把较大的值存储在 LSM-tree 外部，并通过附加在键上的定宽指针指向这些值 [26]。为便于展示，本文所有图中只画出了键，但可以将其视为键值对。

**缓冲更新。** 图 2 展示了一棵 LSM-tree，并列出了全文使用的术语。从概念上说，LSM-tree 由 $L$ 层组成。第 0 层是内存缓冲区，其余层则是二级存储中的数据。LSM-tree 为插入、更新和删除（下文统称为更新）而优化：它把这些操作立即存入第 0 层缓冲区，无需访问二级存储（每个条目都附有一个标志，用来指示它是否为删除操作）。如果一次更新针对的键已经存在于缓冲区中，原条目就会被原地替换，仅保留最新条目。

当缓冲区达到容量上限时，其中的条目会按键排序成数组，并被刷写到二级存储的第 1 层。我们将这种数组称为 run。分配给缓冲区的主存位数记为 $M_{buffer}$，定义为 $M_{buffer} = P \cdot B \cdot E$，其中 $B$ 是一个磁盘页能容纳的条目数，$P$ 是以磁盘页为单位分配给缓冲区的主存量，$E$ 是数据条目的平均大小。例如，LevelDB 的默认缓冲区大小为 2 MB。

第 1 层及更高层的 run 都是不可变的。第 $i$ 层的容量阈值为 $B \cdot P \cdot T^i$ 个条目，其中调优参数 $T$ 表示相邻层容量的大小比。因此，各层容量以 $T$ 为因子呈指数增长。总层数由公式 1 给出。

$$L = \left\lceil \log_T\left(\frac{N \cdot E}{M_{buffer}} \cdot \frac{T-1}{T}\right) \right\rceil \tag{1}$$

大小比 $T$ 有一个极限值 $T_{lim}$，其中 $T_{lim} = \frac{N \cdot E}{M_{buffer}}$。$T$ 可以设置为 2 到 $T_{lim}$ 之间的任意值。当 $T$ 趋近 $T_{lim}$ 时，层数 $L$ 趋近于 1。

**合并操作。** 为限制一次查询需要探查的 run 数量，LSM-tree 根据 run 的大小将其组织到不同层中，并合并大小相近（即位于同一层）的 run。存在两种合并策略：leveling 和 tiering [20]。前者更偏向优化查询，后者更偏向优化更新。在 leveling 中，第 $i$ 层最多有一个 run；从第 $i - 1$ 层移动到第 $i$ 层的任何 run，如果第 $i$ 层已有 run，都会立即与其进行排序合并。在 tiering 中，第 $i$ 层最多可以积累 $T$ 个 run；达到这一数量后，这些 run 才会进行排序合并。二者的本质区别在于，leveled LSM-tree 更积极地合并 run，因此能对一次查询需要探查的 run 总数给出更严格的上界，但代价是更高的摊销更新成本。图 3 对比了大小比 $T$ 设为 3 时 tiering 与 leveling 的合并行为。

*图 3：大小比 $T$ 设为 3，且缓冲区能够容纳的条目数 $B \cdot P$ 设为 2 时，tiered 与 leveled LSM-tree 在一次递归合并前后的状态。*

如果正在进行排序合并的多个 run 中包含相同键的条目，则只保留最近创建（最年轻）的 run 中的条目，因为它是最新的。因此，合并得到的 run 可能小于原始各 run 大小之和。合并操作完成后，如果第 $i$ 层已达到容量上限，生成的 run 就会移动到第 $i + 1$ 层。

**查询。** 点查询从缓冲区开始，按层号从低到高遍历各层（对于 tiering，还会在层内按 run 从新到旧的顺序遍历）。找到第一个匹配条目时，查询便终止。无需继续查找，因为更老 run 中具有相同键的条目已被覆盖。零结果查询（即目标键不存在）可能产生很高的 I/O 成本，因为它会探查所有层中的所有 run。相比之下，范围查询需要对键范围重叠的所有 run 进行排序合并，以识别并忽略已被覆盖的条目。

**探查一个 run。** 在 1996 年提出的原始 LSM-tree 设计 [27] 中，每个 run 被组织成一棵紧凑的 B-tree。然而，过去二十年中主存变得更加廉价，因此现代设计只需在主存中存储一组 fence pointer，为每个 run 的每个磁盘页维护最小值/最大值信息 [15, 19]。维护扁平数组结构简单得多，并且能在内存中取得良好搜索性能（由于每个 run 已排序，可以使用二分查找）。鉴于 LSM-tree 位于磁盘上，这些内存二分查找并不处于性能关键路径上，I/O 才是。因此，查询首先搜索 fence pointer。如果是点查询，接着用一次 I/O 读取相应磁盘页；如果是范围查询，则从该页开始扫描。fence pointer 的大小并不大。例如，使用 16 KB 磁盘页和 4 字节指针时，fence pointer 比原始数据小约 4 个数量级。形式化地说，我们将 fence pointer 占用的主存量记为 $M_{pointers}$，并在全文中假设 $M_{pointers}$ 为 $O(\frac{N}{B})$，由此保证点查询探查一个 run 只需 $O(1)$ 次磁盘 I/O。

**Bloom Filter。** 为加速点查询，每个 run 都在主存中有一个对应的 Bloom filter [10]。点查询在访问二级存储中的 run 之前，先探查该 run 的过滤器。如果过滤器返回阴性，目标键就不在该 run 中，因此查询会跳过对该 run 的访问，节省一次 I/O。如果过滤器返回阳性，目标键可能存在于该 run 中，因此查询会以一次 I/O 的成本探查该 run。如果该 run 确实包含目标键，查询就终止。否则，便出现一次“假阳性”，查询继续探查下一个 run。假阳性会增加查询的 I/O 成本。假阳性率（FPR）取决于：(1) run 中的条目数量；(2) 在主存中分配给该 run 过滤器的位数。二者的关系由下式给出 [32][^2]。

$$FPR = e^{-\frac{bits}{entries} \cdot \ln(2)^2} \tag{2}$$

据我们所知，所有基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储都为所有 Bloom filter 使用相同的每条目位数。这意味着，一次查询平均会探查 $O(e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ 比例的 run，其中 $M_{filters}$ 是分配给所有过滤器的主存总量。当 $M_{filters}$ 分别趋近于 0 或无穷大时，$O(e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ 分别趋近于 1 或 0。我们所知的所有实现都默认为 Bloom filter 的每个条目分配 10 位 [3, 4, 5, 15, 19, 29]，相应的假阳性率约为 1%。采用这一设置时，如果平均条目大小为实践中常见的 128 字节 [2]，Bloom filter 会比原始数据小约 2 个数量级。

**成本分析。** 下面分析 tiered 和 leveled LSM-tree 中更新与查询的最坏情况 I/O 成本复杂度。首先说明这些成本的度量方式。对于更新，我们度量*摊销最坏情况 I/O 成本*，它将一个条目在更新之后参与的合并操作计入在内。对于查询，我们度量*零结果查询的平均最坏情况 I/O 成本*，即对 LSM-tree 中不存在的键执行查询时，预期执行的 I/O 次数。我们关注零结果查询，因为：(1) 它们在实践中十分常见 [11, 29]（例如“若不存在则插入”的查询 [29]）；(2) 它们会产生最大的纯 I/O 开销（即未找到与查询相关条目的读 I/O）。除非另有说明，本文其余部分使用的“最坏情况查询”和“最坏情况更新”均遵循上述定义。为完整起见，后文也会对非零结果查询和范围查询的最坏情况成本建模。

对于 tiered LSM-tree，最坏情况查询成本为 $O(L \cdot T \cdot e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ 次 I/O，因为它有 $O(L)$ 层，每层有 $O(T)$ 个 run；借助 fence pointer，探查每个 run 的成本为一次 I/O；并且平均只需探查 $O(e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ 比例的 run。最坏情况更新成本为 $O(\frac{L}{B})$ 次 I/O，因为每个条目会参与 $O(L)$ 次合并操作，即每层一次；而合并过程中复制一个条目的 I/O 成本为 $O(\frac{1}{B})$，因为每次写 I/O 会把 $O(B)$ 个条目复制到新 run 中。

对于 leveled LSM-tree，最坏情况查询成本为 $O(L \cdot e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ 次 I/O，因为它有 $O(L)$ 层，每层有一个 run；借助 fence pointer，探查一个 run 的成本为一次 I/O；平均会探查 $O(e^{-\frac{M_{filters}}{N}})$ 比例的 run。最坏情况更新成本为 $O(\frac{T \cdot L}{B})$，因为每次更新在每一层会被复制 $O(T)$ 次，并且总共要经过 $O(L)$ 层。

[^2]: 公式 2 假设 Bloom filter 使用能最小化假阳性率的最优哈希函数数量，即 $\frac{bits}{entries} \ln(2)$。

sections.title: 3. LSM-TREE DESIGN SPACE
sections.originalMarkdown:
In this section, we describe critical trade-offs and tuning contentions in the LSM-tree design space. Our contributions in this paper are enabled by a detailed mapping of this space. We introduce a visualization for the design space, and we use this visualization to identify contentions among tuning knobs. In the next section, we resolve these contentions with the design of Monkey.

A summary of the design space of LSM-trees and the impact on lookup and update costs is shown in Figure 4. These costs depend on multiple tuning parameters: (1) the merge policy (tiering vs. leveling), (2) the size ratio $T$ between levels, (3) the allocation of main memory among the buffer $M_{buffer}$ and the Bloom filters, and (4) the allocation of $M_{filters}$ among each of the different Bloom filters. The main observation from Figure 4 is that:

*The design space of LSM trees spans everything between a write-optimized log to a read-optimized sorted array.*

The question is how can we accurately navigate this design space and what is the exact impact of each design decision? To approach an answer to these questions we move on to discuss Figure 4 and the contention among the various design decisions in more detail.

**Tuning the Merge Policy and Size Ratio.** The first insight about the design space is that when the size ratio $T$ is set to 2, the complexities of lookup and update costs for tiering and leveling become identical. As we increase $T$ with tiering/leveling respectively, update cost decreases/increases whereas lookup cost increases/decreases. To generate Figure 4, we plugged all combinations of the merge policy and the size ratio into the complexity equations in Rows 2 and 3 of the table, and we plotted point lookup cost against update cost for corresponding values of the merge policy and size ratio. We did not plot the curve to scale, and in reality the markers are much closer to the graph’s origin. However, the shape of the curve and its limits are accurate. The dotted and solid lines correspond to partitions of the design space that are accessible using tiering and leveling respectively. These lines meet when the size ratio $T$ is set to 2, and they grow farther apart in opposing directions as $T$ increases. This shows that tiering and leveling are complementary methods for navigating the same trade-off continuum.

As the size ratio $T$ approaches its limit value $T_{lim}$, the number of levels $L$ approaches 1. When $L$ is 1, a tiered LSM-tree degenerates to log (left top in the graph of Figure 4) while a leveled LSM-tree degenerates to sorted array (right bottom in the graph of Figure 4). A log is an update-friendly data structure while a sorted array is a read-friendly one. In this way, an LSM-tree can be tuned anywhere between these two extremes in terms of their performance properties. This is a characteristic of the design space we bring forward and heavily utilize in this paper.

*Figure 4: LSM-tree design space: from a log to a sorted array.*

**Tuning Main Memory Allocation.** The limits of the curve in Figure 4 are determined by the allocation of main memory among the filters $M_{filters}$ and the buffer $M_{buffer}$. Getting the memory allocation right is of critical importance. Main memory today is composed of DRAM chips, which cost $\approx 2$ orders of magnitude more than disk in terms of price per bit, and this ratio is increasing as an industry trend [24]. Moreover, DRAM consumes $\approx 4$ times more power per bit than disk during runtime [33]. As a result, the main memory occupied by the Bloom filters and buffer accounts for a significant portion of a system’s (infrastructure and running) cost and should be carefully utilized.

**Design Space Contentions.** Overall, we identify three critical performance contentions in the LSM-tree design space.

**Contention 1** arises in how we allocate a given amount of main memory $M_{filters}$ among the different Bloom filters. By reallocating main memory from one filter to another, we reduce and increase the false positive rates of the former and latter filters respectively. How do we optimally allocate $M_{filters}$ among the different Bloom filters to minimize lookup cost?

**Contention 2** arises in how to allocate the available main memory between the buffer and the filters. As indicated by the complexity table in Figure 4, allocating a more main memory to the buffer on one hand decreases both lookup cost and update cost, but on the other hand it decreases the Bloom filters’ accuracy thereby increasing lookup cost. How do we strike the best balance?

**Contention 3** arises in how to tune the size ratio and merge policy. This is complicated because workloads consist of different proportions of (1) updates, (2) zero-result lookups, (3) non-zero-result lookups, and (4) range lookups of different selectivities. Decreasing the size ratio under tiering and increasing the size ratio under leveling improves lookup cost and degrades update cost, but the impact and rate of change on the costs of different operation types is different. How do we find the best size ratio and merge policy for a particular application workload?

**The State of the Art.** All LSM-tree based key-value stores that we know of apply static and suboptimal decisions regarding the above contentions. The Bloom filters are all tuned the same, the Buffer size relative to the Bloom filters size is static, and the size ratio and merge policy are also static [3, 4, 5, 15, 19, 29]. We refer to these stores collectively as the state of the art. Although they differ from each other in various respects (e.g. centralized vs. decentralized architectures, different consistency guarantees, different data models, etc), these design aspects are orthogonal to this work. In the next section, we introduce Monkey, which improves upon the state of the art by resolving these contentions and being able to quickly, accurately, and optimally navigate the LSM-tree design space.
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节介绍 LSM-tree 设计空间中的关键权衡与调优冲突。本文的贡献建立在对这一空间进行详细映射的基础之上。我们为该设计空间引入一种可视化表示，并利用它识别调优旋钮之间的冲突。在下一节中，我们将通过 Monkey 的设计来解决这些冲突。

图 4 总结了 LSM-tree 的设计空间及其对查询和更新成本的影响。这些成本取决于多个调优参数：(1) 合并策略（tiering 与 leveling）；(2) 层间大小比 $T$；(3) 主存在缓冲区 $M_{buffer}$ 与 Bloom filter 之间的分配；(4) $M_{filters}$ 在各个不同 Bloom filter 之间的分配。图 4 所揭示的主要结论是：

*LSM-tree 的设计空间覆盖了从写优化日志到读优化有序数组之间的一切形态。*

问题在于：如何准确地在这一设计空间中导航，每项设计决策的确切影响又是什么？为了逐步回答这些问题，下面将更详细地讨论图 4 以及各种设计决策之间的冲突。

**调节合并策略和大小比。** 关于该设计空间的第一个洞见是：当大小比 $T$ 设为 2 时，tiering 和 leveling 的查询与更新成本复杂度相同。当分别在 tiering/leveling 下增大 $T$ 时，更新成本会降低/升高，而查询成本则会升高/降低。为生成图 4，我们将合并策略和大小比的所有组合代入表格第 2、3 行的复杂度公式，并针对合并策略与大小比的相应取值，绘制点查询成本相对于更新成本的关系。图中的曲线并未按比例绘制，实际标记点距离图的原点要近得多。不过，曲线的形状及其极限是准确的。虚线和实线分别对应使用 tiering 和 leveling 能够到达的设计空间区域。当大小比 $T$ 设为 2 时，这两条线相交；随着 $T$ 增大，二者沿相反方向越离越远。这表明，tiering 和 leveling 是在同一个权衡连续体中导航的两种互补方法。

当大小比 $T$ 趋近其极限值 $T_{lim}$ 时，层数 $L$ 趋近于 1。当 $L$ 为 1 时，tiered LSM-tree 退化成日志（图 4 左上角），而 leveled LSM-tree 退化成有序数组（图 4 右下角）。日志是一种对更新友好的数据结构，而有序数组是一种对读取友好的数据结构。这样一来，就性能特性而言，LSM-tree 可以被调节到这两个极端之间的任意位置。这正是我们在本文中揭示并大量利用的设计空间特性。

*图 4：LSM-tree 的设计空间：从日志到有序数组。*

**调节主存分配。** 图 4 中曲线的边界由主存在过滤器 $M_{filters}$ 与缓冲区 $M_{buffer}$ 之间的分配决定。正确分配内存至关重要。如今的主存由 DRAM 芯片构成；按每位价格计算，DRAM 比磁盘贵约 2 个数量级，而且这一比值呈持续上升的行业趋势 [24]。此外，运行期间 DRAM 每位消耗的功率约为磁盘的 4 倍 [33]。因此，Bloom filter 和缓冲区占用的主存构成了系统成本（基础设施成本和运行成本）的很大一部分，必须谨慎使用。

**设计空间冲突。** 总体而言，我们在 LSM-tree 设计空间中识别出了三项关键的性能冲突。

**冲突 1** 出现在如何把给定数量的主存 $M_{filters}$ 分配给不同 Bloom filter 的问题上。将主存从一个过滤器重新分配给另一个过滤器，会分别降低前者的假阳性率、提高后者的假阳性率。应如何在不同 Bloom filter 之间以最优方式分配 $M_{filters}$，从而最小化查询成本？

**冲突 2** 出现在如何把可用主存分配给缓冲区和过滤器的问题上。如图 4 的复杂度表所示，给缓冲区分配更多主存，一方面会同时降低查询成本和更新成本；另一方面却会降低 Bloom filter 的准确率，从而提高查询成本。应如何取得最佳平衡？

**冲突 3** 出现在如何调节大小比和合并策略的问题上。该问题非常复杂，因为工作负载由不同比例的以下操作构成：(1) 更新；(2) 零结果查询；(3) 非零结果查询；(4) 具有不同选择率的范围查询。在 tiering 下减小大小比、在 leveling 下增大大小比，都会改善查询成本并恶化更新成本；但对不同操作类型成本的影响和变化速率并不相同。针对某个特定应用工作负载，应如何找到最佳的大小比与合并策略？

**当前先进方法。** 据我们所知，所有基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储面对上述冲突时都采用静态且次优的决策。所有 Bloom filter 的调优方式都相同，缓冲区大小相对于 Bloom filter 大小的比例是静态的，大小比和合并策略也都是静态的 [3, 4, 5, 15, 19, 29]。我们将这些存储统称为当前先进方法。尽管它们在很多方面彼此不同（例如集中式与去中心化架构、不同的一致性保证、不同的数据模型等），这些设计方面与本文的工作正交。在下一节中，我们将介绍 Monkey；它通过解决这些冲突，并能够快速、准确且最优地在 LSM-tree 设计空间中导航，对当前先进方法作出改进。

sections.title: 4. MONKEY
sections.originalMarkdown:
In this section, we present Monkey in detail. Monkey is an LSM-tree based key-value store whose novelty is being able to reach and navigate the Pareto curve to find the best possible balance between the costs of lookups and updates for any given main memory budget, workload and storage medium. It maximizes throughput for uniformly random workloads, and it maximizes the lower-bound on throughput for all other workloads. Monkey achieves this by (1) resolving the contentions in the LSM-tree design space, and (2) using models to optimally trade among lookup cost, update cost, and main memory footprint. Below we give a high level summary of the main design elements and performance impact of Monkey before we move forward to describe each one of them in depth.

**Figure 5:** The design of Monkey impacts performance in three ways: (a), it reaches the Pareto curve by optimally tuning the Bloom filters, (b) it predicts how changes in environmental parameters and main memory utilization reposition the Pareto curve, and (c) it finds the point on the Pareto curve that maximizes worst-case throughput for a given application.

**Design Knobs.** Monkey transforms the design elements that impact worst-case behavior into tuning knobs, and it can alter its behavior by adjusting them. Those knobs comprise: (1) the size ratio among levels $T$, (2) the merge policy (leveling vs. tiering), (3) the false positive rates $p_1...p_L$ assigned to Bloom filters across different levels, and (4) the allocation of main memory $M$ between the buffer $M_{buffer}$ and the filters $M_{filters}$. Monkey can co-tune these knobs to optimize throughput, or in order to favor one performance metric over another if needed by the application (e.g., a bound on average lookup or update latency). Figure 5 shows the performance effects achieved by Monkey.

**Minimizing Lookup Cost.** The first core design element in Monkey is optimal allocation of main memory across Bloom filters to minimize lookup cost. The key insight is that lookup cost is proportional to the sum of the false positive rates (FPR) of all the Bloom filters. In Section 4.1, we show how to tune filters across levels differently to minimize this sum. Figure 5 (a) shows visually the impact this change brings. It achieves faster reads than the state of the art for any main memory budget, and so it shifts the entire trade-off curve vertically down to the Pareto curve.

**Performance Prediction.** The second design element in Monkey is the ability to predict how changing a design decision or an environmental parameter would impact worst-case performance. We achieve this in Section 4.2 by deriving closed-form models for the worst-case I/O costs of lookups and updates in terms of the LSM-tree design space knobs. For instance, the models predict how changing the overall amount of main memory or its allocation would reposition the Pareto curve, as shown Figure 5 (b). We derive an analogous model for the state of the art as a baseline and show that Monkey dominates it.

**Autotuning.** The third design element in Monkey is the ability to holistically self-tune to maximize the worst-case throughput. We achieve this in two steps. First, in Section 4.3 we use asymptotic analysis to map the design space and thereby devise a rule for how to allocate main memory between the buffer and the filters. Second, in Section 4.4 we model worst-case throughput with respect to (1) our models for lookup cost and update cost, (2) the proportion of lookups and updates in the workload, and (3) the costs of reads and writes to persistent storage. We introduce an algorithm that quickly searches the Pareto curve for the balance between lookup and update cost that maximizes the worst-case throughput. This property is visually shown in Figure 5 (c).

**Using Monkey.** We present Monkey as a key-value store. Our work, though, is applicable to any kind of application where one would use an LSM-tree (see discussion on this in Appendix A).
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节详细介绍 Monkey。Monkey 是一种基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储，其创新之处在于：对于任意给定的主存预算、工作负载和存储介质，它都能到达帕累托曲线并沿曲线进行导航，从而在查找成本与更新成本之间找到尽可能好的平衡。对于均匀随机工作负载，它能够最大化吞吐量；对于其他所有工作负载，它能够最大化吞吐量的下界。Monkey 通过以下两点实现这一目标：(1) 解决 LSM-tree 设计空间中的相互制约；(2) 使用模型在查找成本、更新成本和主存占用之间进行最优权衡。在深入描述各个设计要素之前，下面先高度概括 Monkey 的主要设计要素及其性能影响。

**图 5：** Monkey 的设计从三个方面影响性能：(a) 通过对 Bloom 过滤器进行最优调优到达帕累托曲线；(b) 预测环境参数和主存使用方式的变化将如何重新定位帕累托曲线；(c) 找到帕累托曲线上使给定应用的最坏情况吞吐量最大化的点。

**调优旋钮。** Monkey 将影响最坏情况行为的设计要素转化为调优旋钮，并可通过调整这些旋钮改变自身行为。这些旋钮包括：(1) 层之间的大小比率 $T$；(2) 合并策略（leveling 或 tiering）；(3) 分配给不同层 Bloom 过滤器的假阳性率 $p_1...p_L$；以及 (4) 主存 $M$ 在缓冲区 $M_{buffer}$ 与过滤器 $M_{filters}$ 之间的分配。Monkey 可以联合调节这些旋钮以优化吞吐量；如果应用需要偏重某一性能指标，例如对平均查找延迟或更新延迟设置上限，也可以据此进行调节。图 5 展示了 Monkey 所取得的性能效果。

**最小化查找成本。** Monkey 的第一个核心设计要素，是在各 Bloom 过滤器之间最优分配主存，以最小化查找成本。关键洞见是，查找成本与所有 Bloom 过滤器的假阳性率（FPR）之和成正比。在第 4.1 节中，我们说明如何对不同层的过滤器采用不同调优方式，从而最小化这个总和。图 5(a) 直观展示了这一改变带来的影响：对于任意主存预算，它都能获得比现有最佳方案更快的读取速度，因此会将整条权衡曲线竖直下移至帕累托曲线。

**性能预测。** Monkey 的第二个设计要素，是能够预测设计决策或环境参数的改变会如何影响最坏情况性能。第 4.2 节通过推导关于 LSM-tree 设计空间旋钮的查找和更新最坏情况 I/O 成本闭式模型来实现这一点。例如，这些模型可以预测主存总量或其分配方式的改变将如何重新定位帕累托曲线，如图 5(b) 所示。我们还为现有最佳方案推导了一个类似模型作为基线，并证明 Monkey 优于它。

**自动调优。** Monkey 的第三个设计要素，是能够进行整体自调优，以最大化最坏情况吞吐量。我们分两步实现这一点。首先，第 4.3 节使用渐近分析来刻画设计空间，并由此设计出在缓冲区与过滤器之间分配主存的规则。其次，第 4.4 节依据以下因素对最坏情况吞吐量建模：(1) 查找成本和更新成本模型；(2) 工作负载中查找与更新的比例；(3) 对持久存储进行读写的成本。我们提出一种算法，它可以快速搜索帕累托曲线，找到能使最坏情况吞吐量最大化的查找成本与更新成本平衡点。图 5(c) 直观展示了这一性质。

**使用 Monkey。** 我们将 Monkey 表述为一种键值存储。不过，我们的工作适用于任何会使用 LSM-tree 的应用（相关讨论见附录 A）。

sections.title: 4.1 Minimizing Lookup Cost
sections.originalMarkdown:
We now continue to discuss how Monkey minimizes the worst-case lookup cost. We focus for now on zero-result lookups since they incur the highest possible pure I/O overhead (i.e., I/Os that do no useful work), and so minimizing their worst-case cost allows achieving robust performance, and we later show experimentally that it also significantly improves performance in other cases (e.g., non-zero-result lookups and workload skew).

Figure 6 gives a list of terms that we use to describe Monkey. We denote $R$ as the worst-case expected I/O cost of a zero-result point lookup. We first show that $R$ is equal to the sum of the false positive rates (FPRs) of all Bloom filters. We then show how to tune the Bloom filters’ FPRs across different levels to minimize this sum, subject to a constraint on the overall amount of main memory $M_{filters}$. We assume a fixed entry size $E$ throughout this section; in Appendix C we give a iterative optimization algorithm that quickly finds the optimal FPR assignment even when the entry size is variable or changes over time.

| Term | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $p_i$ | False positive rate (FPR) for filters at level $i$ | |
| $R$ | Worst-case zero-result point lookup cost | I/O |
| $R_{filtered}$ | Worst-case zero-result point lookup cost to levels with filters | I/O |
| $R_{unfiltered}$ | Worst-case zero-result point lookup cost to levels with no filters | I/O |
| $V$ | Worst-case non-zero-result point lookup cost | I/O |
| $W$ | Worst-case update cost | I/O |
| $Q$ | Worst-case range lookup cost | I/O |
| $M_{threshold}$ | Value of $M_{filters}$ below which $p_L$ (FPR at level $L$) converges to 1 | bits |
| $\phi$ | Cost ratio between a write and a read I/O to persistent storage | |
| $s$ | Proportion of entries in a range lookup | |
| $L_{filtered}$ | Number of levels with Bloom filters | |
| $L_{unfiltered}$ | Number of levels without Bloom filters | |
| $\hat{M}$ | Amount of main memory to divide among filters and buffer | bits |

**Figure 6:** Overview of how Monkey allocates false positive rates $p_1, p_2...p_L$ to Bloom filters in proportion to the number of key-value pairs in a given level, and a further list of terms used to describe Monkey.

**Modeling Average Worst-Case Lookup Cost.** The average number (i.e., expected value) of runs probed by a zero-result lookup is the sum of the FPRs of all Bloom filters. Equation 3 expresses this sum in terms of the FPRs $p_1...p_L$ assigned to filters at different levels. With leveling every level has at most one run and so $R$ is simply equal to the sum of FPRs across all levels. With tiering there are at most $T-1$ runs at every level (when the $T$th run arrives from the previous level it triggers a merge operation and a push to the next level). The FPR for all runs at the same level in tiering is the same because they have the same size.

$$
R = \begin{cases}
\sum_{i=1}^{L}p_i, & \text{with leveling}\\
(T-1)\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{L}p_i, & \text{with tiering}
\end{cases}
\quad \text{where }0<p_i\leq 1
\tag{3}
$$

**Modeling Main Memory Footprint.** We now model the total main memory footprint for the bloom filters in terms of the FPRs $p_1,p_2...p_L$ of the different levels. To do so, we first rearrange Equation 2 in terms of the number of bits in a filter: $bits=-entries\cdot\frac{\ln(FPR)}{\ln(2)^2}$. This equation captures the cumulative size of any number of Bloom filters that have the same FPRs, and so we can apply it out-of-the-box for both leveling (one Bloom filter per level) and for tiering ($T-1$ Bloom filters per level). We do so by identifying and plugging in the number of entries and the FPR for each level. As shown in Figure 2, the last level of an LSM-tree has at most $N\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}$ entries, and in general Level $i$ has at most $\frac{N}{T^{L-i}}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}$ entries because smaller levels have exponentially smaller capacities by a factor of $T$. Thus, the amount of main memory occupied by filters at Level $i$ is at most $-\frac{N}{T^{L-i}}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot\frac{\ln(p_i)}{\ln(2)^2}$ bits. The overall amount of main memory allocated cumulatively to all Bloom filters is the sum of this expression over all levels, as captured by Equation 4.

$$
M_{filters}=-\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{L}\frac{\ln(p_i)}{T^{L-i}}
\tag{4}
$$

**Minimizing Lookup Cost with Monkey.** Using Equations 3 and 4, we can calculate the average worst-case lookup cost $R$ and main memory footprint $M_{filters}$ for any assignment of FPRs across the different levels. To minimize $R$ with respect to $M_{filters}$, we first tackle the converse yet equivalent problem for ease of exposition: finding the optimal assignment of FPRs $p_1...p_L$ across the different levels that minimizes $M_{filters}$ for any user-specified value of $R$. This amounts to a multivariate constrained optimization problem. In Appendix B, we solve it by applying the method of Lagrange Multipliers on Equations 3 and 4. The result appears in Equations 5 and 6 for leveled and tiered designs respectively.³

**Leveling**

$$
p_i=\begin{cases}
1,&\text{if }i>L_{filtered}\\
\frac{(R-L_{unfiltered})(T-1)}{T^{L_{filtered}+1-i}},&\text{else}
\end{cases}
$$

for $0<R\leq L$ and $1\leq i\leq L$, and $L_{filtered}=L-\max(0,\lfloor R-1\rfloor)$. (5)

**Tiering**

$$
p_i=\begin{cases}
1,&\text{if }i>L_{filtered}\\
\frac{R-L_{unfiltered}(T-1)}{T^{L_{filtered}+1-i}},&\text{else}
\end{cases}
$$

for $0<R\leq L\cdot(T-1)$ and $1\leq i\leq L$, and $L_{filtered}=L-\max(0,\lfloor\frac{R-1}{T-1}\rfloor)$. (6)

Figure 6 illustrates how Monkey optimally assigns FPRs to Bloom filters across different levels using Equations 5 and 6. In general, the optimal FPR at Level $i$ is $T$ times higher than the optimal FPR at Level $i-1$. In other words, the optimal FPR for level $i$ is proportional to the number of elements at level $i$. The intuition is that the I/O cost of probing any run is the same regardless of its size (due to the fence pointers we only fetch the qualifying disk page), yet the amount of main memory needed for achieving a low FPR at deeper levels is significantly higher since they have exponentially more entries. It is therefore better to set relatively more bits per entry (i.e., a lower FPR) to the filters at smaller levels. In contrast, state-of-the-art LSM-based key-value stores assign the same FPR to Bloom filters across all the different levels.

The higher we set the lookup cost $R$, the less main memory for the Bloom filters we need. As shown in Figure 6, the mechanism through which this works in Monkey is that for higher values of $R$ more of the Bloom filters at the deepest levels cease to exist as their optimal FPRs converge to 1. We denote the number of levels with and without filters as $L_{filtered}$ and $L_{unfiltered}$ respectively (note that $L=L_{filtered}+L_{unfiltered}$). Equations 5 and 6 are adapted to find the optimal division between $L_{filtered}$ and $L_{unfiltered}$ and to prescribe FPRs to the shallowest $L_{filtered}$ levels based on smaller version of the problem with $L_{filtered}$ levels.

In summary, Monkey minimizes the main memory footprint for the Bloom filters for a given lookup cost $R$ by (1) finding the optimal number of levels $L_{filtered}$ to which Bloom filters should be allocated, and (2) setting the FPR for each of these levels to be proportional to its capacity. Through these steps it achieves the performance effect shown in Figure 5 (a).

³ In the next subsection, we show how to express Equations 5 and 6 in terms of $M_{filters}$ rather than $R$.
sections.translationMarkdown:
下面继续讨论 Monkey 如何最小化最坏情况查找成本。我们目前重点考察零结果查找，因为它会产生尽可能高的纯 I/O 开销，即不完成任何有用工作的 I/O。因此，最小化其最坏情况成本可以获得稳健的性能；后文的实验还会表明，这也能显著改善其他情形下的性能，例如非零结果查找和工作负载偏斜。

图 6 列出了描述 Monkey 时使用的术语。我们用 $R$ 表示零结果点查找在最坏情况下的预期 I/O 成本。首先证明 $R$ 等于所有 Bloom 过滤器假阳性率（FPR）之和；然后说明，在主存总量 $M_{filters}$ 的约束下，如何调节不同层 Bloom 过滤器的 FPR 来最小化这个总和。本节始终假设条目大小 $E$ 固定；附录 C 给出了一种迭代优化算法，即使条目大小可变或随时间变化，也能快速找出最优的 FPR 分配。

| 术语 | 说明 | 单位 |
|---|---|---|
| $p_i$ | 第 $i$ 层过滤器的假阳性率（FPR） | |
| $R$ | 零结果点查找的最坏情况成本 | I/O |
| $R_{filtered}$ | 对有过滤器层执行零结果点查找的最坏情况成本 | I/O |
| $R_{unfiltered}$ | 对无过滤器层执行零结果点查找的最坏情况成本 | I/O |
| $V$ | 非零结果点查找的最坏情况成本 | I/O |
| $W$ | 最坏情况更新成本 | I/O |
| $Q$ | 最坏情况范围查找成本 | I/O |
| $M_{threshold}$ | 使第 $L$ 层过滤器的 $p_L$（FPR）趋近于 1 的 $M_{filters}$ 下界值 | 位 |
| $\phi$ | 对持久存储执行一次写 I/O 与一次读 I/O 的成本比 | |
| $s$ | 范围查找所包含的条目比例 | |
| $L_{filtered}$ | 具有 Bloom 过滤器的层数 | |
| $L_{unfiltered}$ | 没有 Bloom 过滤器的层数 | |
| $\hat{M}$ | 在过滤器和缓冲区之间分配的主存总量 | 位 |

**图 6：** Monkey 如何按照给定层中键值对的数量，成比例地向 Bloom 过滤器分配假阳性率 $p_1,p_2...p_L$；图中还列出了描述 Monkey 时使用的术语。

**平均最坏情况查找成本建模。** 零结果查找所探测的 run 的平均数量，即期望值，等于所有 Bloom 过滤器 FPR 之和。公式 3 使用分配给不同层过滤器的 FPR $p_1...p_L$ 表示这个总和。采用 leveling 时，每层至多有一个 run，因此 $R$ 就等于所有层 FPR 之和。采用 tiering 时，每层至多有 $T-1$ 个 run；当前一层的第 $T$ 个 run 到达时，会触发合并操作并将结果推入下一层。tiering 中同一层所有 run 的 FPR 相同，因为它们的大小相同。

$$
R = \begin{cases}
\sum_{i=1}^{L}p_i, & \text{采用 leveling}\\
(T-1)\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{L}p_i, & \text{采用 tiering}
\end{cases}
\quad \text{其中 }0<p_i\leq 1
\tag{3}
$$

**主存占用建模。** 现在根据不同层的 FPR $p_1,p_2...p_L$，对 Bloom 过滤器的主存总占用进行建模。为此，首先将公式 2 变形为关于过滤器位数的表达式：$bits=-entries\cdot\frac{\ln(FPR)}{\ln(2)^2}$。该公式给出了具有相同 FPR 的任意数量 Bloom 过滤器的累计大小，因此可以直接用于 leveling（每层一个 Bloom 过滤器）和 tiering（每层 $T-1$ 个 Bloom 过滤器）。具体做法是确定每层的条目数和 FPR，并将其代入公式。如图 2 所示，LSM-tree 最后一层至多有 $N\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}$ 个条目；一般而言，第 $i$ 层至多有 $\frac{N}{T^{L-i}}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}$ 个条目，因为较小层的容量会以 $T$ 为因子呈指数减小。因此，第 $i$ 层过滤器占用的主存至多为 $-\frac{N}{T^{L-i}}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot\frac{\ln(p_i)}{\ln(2)^2}$ 位。分配给所有 Bloom 过滤器的主存总量，就是该表达式在所有层上的总和，如公式 4 所示。

$$
M_{filters}=-\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{T-1}{T}\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{L}\frac{\ln(p_i)}{T^{L-i}}
\tag{4}
$$

**使用 Monkey 最小化查找成本。** 利用公式 3 和公式 4，对于任意跨层 FPR 分配，都可以计算平均最坏情况查找成本 $R$ 和主存占用 $M_{filters}$。为了在给定 $M_{filters}$ 时最小化 $R$，为便于说明，我们先处理一个逆向但等价的问题：对于用户指定的任意 $R$，寻找跨层 FPR $p_1...p_L$ 的最优分配，使 $M_{filters}$ 最小。这是一个多变量约束优化问题。附录 B 对公式 3 和公式 4 应用拉格朗日乘数法求解。对于 leveling 和 tiering 设计，结果分别如公式 5 和公式 6 所示。³

**Leveling**

$$
p_i=\begin{cases}
1,&\text{若 }i>L_{filtered}\\
\frac{(R-L_{unfiltered})(T-1)}{T^{L_{filtered}+1-i}},&\text{否则}
\end{cases}
$$

其中 $0<R\leq L$、$1\leq i\leq L$，并且 $L_{filtered}=L-\max(0,\lfloor R-1\rfloor)$。（5）

**Tiering**

$$
p_i=\begin{cases}
1,&\text{若 }i>L_{filtered}\\
\frac{R-L_{unfiltered}(T-1)}{T^{L_{filtered}+1-i}},&\text{否则}
\end{cases}
$$

其中 $0<R\leq L\cdot(T-1)$、$1\leq i\leq L$，并且 $L_{filtered}=L-\max(0,\lfloor\frac{R-1}{T-1}\rfloor)$。（6）

图 6 展示了 Monkey 如何利用公式 5 和公式 6，向不同层的 Bloom 过滤器最优分配 FPR。一般而言，第 $i$ 层的最优 FPR 是第 $i-1$ 层最优 FPR 的 $T$ 倍。换言之，第 $i$ 层的最优 FPR 与该层元素数量成正比。其直觉在于，无论一个 run 的大小如何，探测该 run 的 I/O 成本都相同；由于有 fence pointer，我们只需读取符合条件的磁盘页。然而，更深层拥有指数级更多的条目，因此要在更深层实现较低 FPR，需要显著更多的主存。所以，最好为较小层的过滤器设置相对更多的每条目位数，即更低的 FPR。相比之下，现有最佳的基于 LSM 的键值存储会为所有不同层的 Bloom 过滤器分配相同 FPR。

我们将查找成本 $R$ 设得越高，Bloom 过滤器所需的主存就越少。如图 6 所示，Monkey 实现这一点的机制是：随着 $R$ 增大，最深处更多 Bloom 过滤器的最优 FPR 会趋近于 1，从而使这些过滤器不再存在。我们分别用 $L_{filtered}$ 和 $L_{unfiltered}$ 表示有过滤器和无过滤器的层数，注意 $L=L_{filtered}+L_{unfiltered}$。公式 5 和公式 6 经过调整，可以找出 $L_{filtered}$ 与 $L_{unfiltered}$ 之间的最优划分，并基于一个只含 $L_{filtered}$ 层的较小问题，为最浅的 $L_{filtered}$ 层指定 FPR。

总之，对于给定的查找成本 $R$，Monkey 通过以下两步最小化 Bloom 过滤器的主存占用：(1) 找出应分配 Bloom 过滤器的最优层数 $L_{filtered}$；(2) 将每一层的 FPR 设置为与其容量成正比。通过这些步骤，它实现了图 5(a) 所示的性能效果。

³ 下一小节将说明如何用 $M_{filters}$ 而非 $R$ 表示公式 5 和公式 6。

sections.title: 4.2 Predicting Lookup and Update Costs
4.3 Scalability and Tunability
sections.originalMarkdown:
We now continue by exploring and mapping the design space for Monkey using asymptotic analysis. We show that lookup cost in Monkey scales better than in the state of the art with respect to data volume. We also show that Monkey removes the dependence of lookup cost on the buffer size thereby simplifying tuning.

**Complexity Analysis.** In Table 1, we express the models for worst-case lookup cost and update cost using big O notations. We do so by applying standard simplification rules on Equations 7 and 8 for $R$ in Monkey, on Equation 26 for lookup cost in the state of the art, and on Equation 10 for update cost in both.

| Merge policy | Update Cost ($W$) | State of the Art Lookup Cost, $M_{filters}\leq M_{threshold}$ | State of the Art Lookup Cost, $M_{threshold}\leq M_{filters}$ | Monkey Lookup Cost ($R$), $\frac{M_{threshold}}{T^L}\leq M_{filters}\leq M_{threshold}$ | Monkey Lookup Cost ($R$), $M_{threshold}\leq M_{filters}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tiering ($T=T_{lim}$) | $O(1/B)$ | $O(N\cdot E/M_{buffer})$ | $O((N\cdot E/M_{buffer})\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ | $O(N\cdot E/M_{buffer})$ | $O((N\cdot E/M_{buffer})\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ |
| Tiering ($2\leq T<T_{lim}$) | $O((1/B)\cdot\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer}))$ | $O(T\cdot\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer}))$ | $O(T\cdot\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer})\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ | $O(T\cdot\log_T(N/M_{filters}))$ | $O(T\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ |
| Leveling ($2\leq T<T_{lim}$) | $O((T/B)\cdot\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer}))$ | $O(\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer}))$ | $O(\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer})\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ | $O(\log_T(N/M_{filters}))$ | $O(e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ |
| Leveling ($T=T_{lim}$) | $O((1/B)\cdot N\cdot E/M_{buffer})$ | $O(1)$ | $O(e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ | $O(1)$ | $O(e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ |

**Table 1:** Asymptotic analysis reveals that (1) lookup cost in Monkey scales better than the state of the art with respect to the number and size of data entries, (2) lookup cost in Monkey is independent of the LSM-tree’s buffer size, and (3) Monkey and the state of the art both degenerate into a log and sorted array with tiering and leveling respectively as the size ratio $T$ is pushed to its limit.

The complexity of worst-case lookup cost for Monkey is different depending on whether $M_{filters}$ is greater or lower than $M_{threshold}$. To understand why, recall that $R$ in Equation 7 is expressed as the sum of two additive terms, $R_{filtered}$ and $R_{unfiltered}$. As long as $M_{filters}>M_{threshold}$, there are filters at all levels and so $R_{unfiltered}$ is zero and $R_{filtered}$ is the dominant term. Moreover, by plugging in $M_{threshold}$ for $M_{filters}$ in Equation 7, we observe that the value of $R_{filtered}$ can be at most $O(1)$ for leveling and at most $O(T)$ for tiering. However, as the number of entries $N$ increases relative to $M_{filters}$, eventually $M_{filters}$ drops below $M_{threshold}$. At this point $R_{unfiltered}$ becomes non-zero and comes to dominate $R_{filtered}$ because its value is at least $O(1)$ with leveling and $O(T)$ with tiering when $M_{filters}=M_{threshold}$, and it increases up to $O(L)$ with leveling and $O(L\cdot T)$ with tiering as $N$ increases. Thus, the complexity of worst-case lookup cost $R$ is $O(R_{filtered})$ when $M_{filters}>M_{threshold}$, and otherwise it is $O(R_{unfiltered})$.

The condition $M_{filters}>M_{threshold}$ can be equivalently stated as having the number of bits per element $\frac{M_{filters}}{N}>\frac{1}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}$. The value of $\frac{1}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}$ is at most 1.44 when $T$ is equal to 2. Hence, we can say more concretely that the complexity of worst-case lookup cost $R$ is $O(R_{filtered})$ when the number of bits-per-element is above 1.44, and otherwise it is $O(R_{unfiltered})$. In modern key-value stores, the number of bits-per-element is typically 10, far above 1.44, and so for most practical purposes the complexity of Monkey is $O(R_{filtered})$.

To enable an apples to apples comparison, we also express the complexity of lookup cost for the state of the art separately for when $M_{filters}$ is lower and greater than $M_{threshold}$ (Column b and c respectively in Table 1). We observe that when $M_{filters}$ is lower than $M_{threshold}$, the complexity of lookup cost converges to that of an LSM-tree with no filters.

**Comparing Monkey to the State of the Art.** We first compare Monkey to the state of the art when $M_{filters}\geq M_{threshold}$ (Columns c and e in Table 1). Monkey shaves a factor of $O(L)$ from the complexity of lookup cost for both tiering and leveling (Rows 2 and 3 in Table 1). Note that we express $O(L)$ in Table 1 as $O(\log_T(\frac{N\cdot E}{M_{buffer}}))$ as per Equation 1. In other words, lookup cost $R$ in Monkey is asymptotically independent of the number of levels $L$ of the LSM-tree. The intuition is that the FPRs for smaller levels are exponentially decreasing, and so the expected cost of probing filters across the levels converges to a multiplicative constant. Shaving a factor of $O(L)$ from lookup cost has three important benefits.

1. As long as we scale the Bloom filters’ footprint with the number of data entries (i.e., keep the ratio $\frac{M_{filters}}{N}$ fixed as $N$ increases), lookup cost in Monkey stays fixed whereas in the state of the art it increases at a logarithmic rate. In this way, Monkey dominates the state of the art by an increasingly large margin as the number of entries increases.
2. Lookup cost is independent of the entry size, and so it does not increase for data sets with larger entry sizes.
3. Lookup cost is independent of the buffer size. This simplifies tuning relative to the state of the art, because we do not need to carefully balance main memory allocation between the buffer and filters to optimize lookup performance.

Next, we compare Monkey to the state of the art when $M_{filters}\leq M_{threshold}$. In the state of the art (Column b, Rows 2 and 3 in Table 1), lookup cost decreases at a logarithmic rate as $M_{buffer}$ increases because it absorbs more of the smaller levels. In Monkey (Column d, Rows 2 and 3), lookup cost decreases at a logarithmic rate as $M_{filters}$ increases since more of the deeper levels have Bloom filters. Monkey improves upon the state of the art by shaving an additive factor of $O(\log_T(E))$ from lookup cost, where $E$ is the entry size. The reason is that Bloom filters are only sensitive to the number of entries rather than their sizes. This means that lookup cost in Monkey does not increase for data sets with larger entries.

Monkey dominates the state of the art for all data sets and tunings because it allocates main memory optimally among the Bloom filters thereby minimizing lookup cost.

**Exploring Limiting Behavior.** We now focus on the limiting behavior of the lookup cost with respect to the size ratio. This is shown in Rows 1 and 4 of Table 1. We also plot Figure 8 to help this discussion. Figure 8 is an extension of Figure 4 from Section 2 where we mapped the design space of LSM-trees with respect to the impact on lookup and update cost. Figure 4 includes Monkey in addition to the existing state of the art. As $T$ approaches its limit value of $T_{lim}$ for both leveling and tiering, the number of levels $L$ approaches 1. When $T=T_{lim}$, Monkey and the state of the art both degenerate into a log and a sorted array with tiering and leveling respectively, and so their performance characteristics converge. For all other values of $T$ in-between, Monkey dominates the state of the art by reducing the lookup cost, hence reaching the Pareto curve.

**Figure 8:** Monkey dominates the state of the art for any merge policy and size ratio.

**Analyzing Main Memory Allocation.** We now analyze the impact of allocating main memory between the filters and buffer on lookup and update cost. In Figure 9, we plot lookup cost and update cost with Monkey and the state of the art as we vary the relative sizes of the buffer and the filters.⁴ We define the amount of main memory excluding the fence pointers that is to be divided between the fence pointers and buffer as $\hat{M}$ bits, and so $\hat{M}=M_{buffer}+M_{filters}$. On the x-axis (log scale), we increase $M_{buffer}$ at the expense of $M_{filters}$ from one disk page ($B\cdot E$ bits) to $\hat{M}$ bits, in which case the Bloom filters cease to exist (i.e., $M_{filters}=0$). While the terms in the figure are general, the curves are drawn using our models in Subsection 4.2 for the configuration outlined at the end of the subsection. The term $X$ is used to adjust the y-axis for leveling and tiering.

The top part of Figure 9 reveals that Bloom filters in the state of the art actually harm lookup performance for a significant portion of the space because the main memory that they occupy is better-off allocated to the buffer. Monkey removes this performance contention thereby simplifying system tuning by making lookup cost independent of the buffer size, provided that $M_{filters}>\frac{M_{threshold}}{T^L}$ as per Equation 8. As the buffer continues to grow, however, the Bloom filters shrink and eventually cease to exist, at which point the curves for Monkey and the state-of-the-art converge. The bottom part of Figure 9 illustrates that increasing the buffer size decreases update cost but incurs diminishing returns as the update cost decreases at a logarithmic rate. The overall insight is that it is desirable to set the buffer size as large as possible to reduce update cost while still keeping it below the point where it begins to significantly harm lookup cost. We mark this in Figure 9 as the “sweet spot”. In the next subsection, we give a strategy for how to allocate main memory among the filters and buffer using this insight.

**Figure 9:** Monkey simplifies tuning by eliminating the dependence of lookup cost on the buffer size.

⁴ The limits on the y-axis are drawn for $M_{filters}>M_{threshold}$.
sections.translationMarkdown:
下面使用渐近分析继续探索并刻画 Monkey 的设计空间。我们将证明，就数据量而言，Monkey 的查找成本比现有最佳方案具有更好的扩展性。我们还将证明，Monkey 消除了查找成本对缓冲区大小的依赖，从而简化了调优。

**复杂度分析。** 表 1 使用大 O 记号表示最坏情况查找成本和更新成本模型。具体方法是：对 Monkey 中 $R$ 的公式 7 和公式 8、现有最佳方案查找成本的公式 26，以及二者更新成本的公式 10 应用标准化简规则。

| 合并策略 | 更新成本（$W$） | 现有最佳方案查找成本，$M_{filters}\leq M_{threshold}$ | 现有最佳方案查找成本，$M_{threshold}\leq M_{filters}$ | Monkey 查找成本（$R$），$\frac{M_{threshold}}{T^L}\leq M_{filters}\leq M_{threshold}$ | Monkey 查找成本（$R$），$M_{threshold}\leq M_{filters}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tiering（$T=T_{lim}$） | $O(1/B)$ | $O(N\cdot E/M_{buffer})$ | $O((N\cdot E/M_{buffer})\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ | $O(N\cdot E/M_{buffer})$ | $O((N\cdot E/M_{buffer})\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ |
| Tiering（$2\leq T<T_{lim}$） | $O((1/B)\cdot\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer}))$ | $O(T\cdot\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer}))$ | $O(T\cdot\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer})\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ | $O(T\cdot\log_T(N/M_{filters}))$ | $O(T\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ |
| Leveling（$2\leq T<T_{lim}$） | $O((T/B)\cdot\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer}))$ | $O(\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer}))$ | $O(\log_T(N\cdot E/M_{buffer})\cdot e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ | $O(\log_T(N/M_{filters}))$ | $O(e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ |
| Leveling（$T=T_{lim}$） | $O((1/B)\cdot N\cdot E/M_{buffer})$ | $O(1)$ | $O(e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ | $O(1)$ | $O(e^{-M_{filters}/N})$ |

**表 1：** 渐近分析表明：(1) 就数据条目的数量和大小而言，Monkey 的查找成本比现有最佳方案具有更好的扩展性；(2) Monkey 的查找成本与 LSM-tree 的缓冲区大小无关；(3) 当大小比率 $T$ 被推至极限时，Monkey 和现有最佳方案在采用 tiering 和 leveling 时都会分别退化为日志和有序数组。

Monkey 的最坏情况查找成本复杂度会因 $M_{filters}$ 高于还是低于 $M_{threshold}$ 而有所不同。为了理解原因，回顾一下公式 7：$R$ 被表示为两个相加项 $R_{filtered}$ 和 $R_{unfiltered}$ 之和。只要 $M_{filters}>M_{threshold}$，所有层都有过滤器，所以 $R_{unfiltered}$ 为零，而 $R_{filtered}$ 是主导项。此外，将 $M_{threshold}$ 作为 $M_{filters}$ 代入公式 7，可以观察到：leveling 下 $R_{filtered}$ 至多为 $O(1)$，tiering 下至多为 $O(T)$。然而，随着条目数 $N$ 相对于 $M_{filters}$ 增大，$M_{filters}$ 最终会降到 $M_{threshold}$ 以下。此时 $R_{unfiltered}$ 变为非零并开始主导 $R_{filtered}$，因为当 $M_{filters}=M_{threshold}$ 时，其值在 leveling 下至少为 $O(1)$，在 tiering 下至少为 $O(T)$；随着 $N$ 增大，它在 leveling 下最多增至 $O(L)$，在 tiering 下最多增至 $O(L\cdot T)$。因此，当 $M_{filters}>M_{threshold}$ 时，最坏情况查找成本 $R$ 的复杂度为 $O(R_{filtered})$；否则为 $O(R_{unfiltered})$。

条件 $M_{filters}>M_{threshold}$ 等价于每元素位数满足 $\frac{M_{filters}}{N}>\frac{1}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}$。当 $T=2$ 时，$\frac{1}{\ln(2)^2}\cdot\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1}$ 的值最大，为 1.44。因此，可以更具体地说：当每元素位数高于 1.44 时，最坏情况查找成本 $R$ 的复杂度为 $O(R_{filtered})$；否则为 $O(R_{unfiltered})$。现代键值存储的每元素位数通常为 10，远高于 1.44，所以对于大多数实际用途，Monkey 的复杂度为 $O(R_{filtered})$。

为了进行同等条件下的比较，我们还分别表示了当 $M_{filters}$ 低于和高于 $M_{threshold}$ 时现有最佳方案的查找成本复杂度，分别对应表 1 的 b 列和 c 列。可以观察到，当 $M_{filters}$ 低于 $M_{threshold}$ 时，查找成本的复杂度会趋近于没有过滤器的 LSM-tree。

**Monkey 与现有最佳方案的比较。** 首先比较 $M_{filters}\geq M_{threshold}$ 时的 Monkey 与现有最佳方案，即表 1 的 c 列和 e 列。对于 tiering 和 leveling，Monkey 都从查找成本复杂度中削减了一个 $O(L)$ 因子，即表 1 的第 2 行和第 3 行。注意，根据公式 1，表 1 将 $O(L)$ 表示为 $O(\log_T(\frac{N\cdot E}{M_{buffer}}))$。换言之，Monkey 的查找成本 $R$ 在渐近意义上与 LSM-tree 的层数 $L$ 无关。其直觉是，较小层的 FPR 呈指数下降，因此跨层探测过滤器的预期成本会收敛到一个乘法常数。从查找成本中消除 $O(L)$ 因子具有三个重要好处。

1. 只要 Bloom 过滤器的占用随数据条目数同比扩展，即随着 $N$ 增大保持 $\frac{M_{filters}}{N}$ 不变，Monkey 的查找成本就保持固定，而现有最佳方案的查找成本会以对数速率增加。这样，随着条目数量增加，Monkey 会以越来越大的优势胜过现有最佳方案。
2. 查找成本与条目大小无关，因此不会因数据集中的条目更大而增加。
3. 查找成本与缓冲区大小无关。与现有最佳方案相比，这简化了调优，因为我们不必为了优化查找性能而仔细平衡缓冲区与过滤器之间的主存分配。

接下来比较 $M_{filters}\leq M_{threshold}$ 时的 Monkey 与现有最佳方案。在现有最佳方案中，即表 1 的 b 列第 2 行和第 3 行，随着 $M_{buffer}$ 增大，缓冲区会吸收更多较小层，因此查找成本以对数速率下降。在 Monkey 中，即 d 列第 2 行和第 3 行，随着 $M_{filters}$ 增大，更多深层拥有 Bloom 过滤器，因此查找成本以对数速率下降。Monkey 从查找成本中削减了一个加法项 $O(\log_T(E))$，从而优于现有最佳方案，其中 $E$ 是条目大小。原因是 Bloom 过滤器只对条目数量敏感，而不对条目大小敏感。这意味着 Monkey 的查找成本不会因数据集中的条目更大而增加。

对于所有数据集和调优配置，Monkey 都优于现有最佳方案，因为它在 Bloom 过滤器之间最优分配主存，从而最小化查找成本。

**探索极限行为。** 现在重点考察查找成本相对于大小比率的极限行为，如表 1 的第 1 行和第 4 行所示。图 8 也有助于理解这一讨论。图 8 是第 2 节图 4 的扩展；图 4 根据查找成本和更新成本受到的影响刻画了 LSM-tree 的设计空间，图 8 则在现有最佳方案之外加入了 Monkey。对于 leveling 和 tiering，当 $T$ 趋近其极限值 $T_{lim}$ 时，层数 $L$ 趋近于 1。当 $T=T_{lim}$ 时，Monkey 与现有最佳方案在 tiering 下都退化为日志，在 leveling 下都退化为有序数组，因此其性能特征会趋同。对于其间的其他所有 $T$ 值，Monkey 都通过降低查找成本而优于现有最佳方案，从而到达帕累托曲线。

**图 8：** 对于任意合并策略和大小比率，Monkey 都优于现有最佳方案。

**分析主存分配。** 现在分析在过滤器和缓冲区之间分配主存对查找成本和更新成本的影响。在图 9 中，我们改变缓冲区和过滤器的相对大小，并绘制 Monkey 与现有最佳方案的查找成本和更新成本。⁴ 将不包括 fence pointer、需要在过滤器和缓冲区之间分配的主存量定义为 $\hat{M}$ 位，因此 $\hat{M}=M_{buffer}+M_{filters}$。在横轴的对数尺度上，我们以牺牲 $M_{filters}$ 为代价，将 $M_{buffer}$ 从一个磁盘页，即 $B\cdot E$ 位，增大到 $\hat{M}$ 位；此时 Bloom 过滤器不复存在，即 $M_{filters}=0$。尽管图中各项是一般形式，但曲线使用第 4.2 小节的模型，按照该小节末尾给出的配置绘制。$X$ 用于针对 leveling 和 tiering 调整纵轴。

图 9 的上半部分揭示，对于设计空间中的相当大一部分，现有最佳方案中的 Bloom 过滤器实际上会损害查找性能，因为它们占用的主存如果分配给缓冲区会更好。只要公式 8 所示的 $M_{filters}>\frac{M_{threshold}}{T^L}$，Monkey 就会使查找成本与缓冲区大小无关，从而消除这种性能制约并简化系统调优。然而，随着缓冲区继续增大，Bloom 过滤器会缩小并最终消失，此时 Monkey 与现有最佳方案的曲线会收敛。图 9 的下半部分说明，增大缓冲区会降低更新成本，但由于更新成本以对数速率下降，收益会递减。总体洞见是：为了降低更新成本，应尽可能增大缓冲区，但仍需使其低于开始显著损害查找成本的点。图 9 将该点标为“甜点”。下一小节将利用这一洞见，给出在过滤器与缓冲区之间分配主存的策略。

**图 9：** Monkey 通过消除查找成本对缓冲区大小的依赖来简化调优。

⁴ 纵轴的界限是在 $M_{filters}>M_{threshold}$ 的条件下绘制的。

sections.title: 4.4 Tuning Based on Workload, Hardware and SLAs
sections.titleTranslation: 4.4 基于工作负载、硬件和 SLA 的调优
sections.originalMarkdown:
We now show how to navigate the LSM-tree design space. Monkey achieves this by being able to precisely trade among lookup cost, update cost, and main memory footprint by controlling four tuning parameters: the merge policy (tiering vs. leveling), the size ratio, the amount of main memory allocated to the filters, and the amount of main memory allocated to the buffer. We show how to tune these parameters with respect to the dataset (number and size of entries), the workload (proportion of lookups and updates), and the storage medium (cost ratio between reads and writes, and size of disk blocks). We model the worst-case throughput in terms of these parameters, and we devise an algorithm that finds the tuning that maximizes throughput. In Table 2 we list new terms.

| Term | Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $r$ | Proportion of zero-result point lookups | |
| $v$ | Proportion of non-zero-result point lookups | |
| $w$ | Proportion of updates | |
| $q$ | Proportion of range lookups | |
| $\hat{M}$ | Main memory to divide between the filters and buffer | bits |
| $\theta$ | Average operation cost in terms of lookups | I/O |
| $\Omega$ | Time to read a page from persistent storage | sec |
| $\tau$ | Worst-case throughput | I/O / sec |

**Table 2:** Table of terms used for tuning.

**Modeling Throughput.** First, we model the average operation cost $\theta$ by weighting the zero-result point lookup cost $R$, the non-zero result point lookup cost $V$, the range lookup cost $Q$, and the update cost $W$ from Equations 7, 9, 10 and 11 by their proportion in the workload represented by the terms $r$, $v$, $q$ and $w$ respectively (note that $r+v+q+w=1$). The result is Equation 12.

$$\theta=r\cdot R+v\cdot V+q\cdot Q+w\cdot W\tag{12}$$

To obtain the worst-case throughput $\tau$, we take the inverse of the average operation cost $\theta$ multiplied by $\Omega$, the amount of time to perform a read to secondary storage. The result is Equation 13.

$$\tau=1/(\theta\cdot\Omega)\tag{13}$$

**Tuning the Size Ratio and Merge Policy.** The merge policy and size ratio are complementary means of navigating the same trade-off continuum. We devise a divide and conquer algorithm that linearizes this continuum into a single dimension and finds the tuning that maximizes throughput. Figure 10 illustrates an example of the first three iterations of the algorithm to find the optimal tuning marked as “target”. In Appendix D we give the full algorithm.

The algorithm first sets $T$ to 2 and computes $\theta$ for this tuning. It then iteratively divides the space into two and computes $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ for the center of each. If either $\theta_1$ or $\theta_2$ is smaller than $\theta$, we narrow down on that partition of the space in the next iteration. Otherwise, we remain at the same point, but we probe points that are twice as close to the current point. This algorithm runs in $O(\log_2(T_{lim}))$ time since there are $O(T_{lim})$ possible tunings and since we halve the size of the problem in each iteration. In practice, it runs in milliseconds.

Using this algorithm, we can also impose upper-bounds on lookup cost or update cost to support different service-level agreements (SLAs). To do so, the algorithm discards configurations for which either lookup or update cost exceeds an imposed upper-bound. This is analogous to restricting the search space by setting a horizontal limit for lookup cost or a vertical limit for update cost in Figure 10.

**Figure 10:** Monkey divides and conquers the design space to find the tuning that maximizes throughput.

**Tuning Main Memory Allocation.** We introduce a three-step strategy for allocating the available main memory $\hat{M}$ between the buffer and filters. Equation 8 shows that when $M_{filters}<\frac{M_{threshold}}{T^L}$ the filters are too small to yield any benefit, and so step one is to always allocate the first $\min(\hat{M},\frac{M_{threshold}}{T^L})$ bits of $\hat{M}$ to the buffer. In step two, we allocate 5% to the buffer and 95% to the filters of the remaining main memory. This method approximates the sweet spot in Figure 9. We continue following step two until the I/O overhead due to false positives becomes negligible (e.g., compared to the CPU and RAM overhead of probing a Bloom filter, which we approximate to be in the order of a microsecond). In a disk-based system, seek latency is $\approx 10$ milliseconds. This means that when the sum of false positives $R$ in Equation 7 reaches the order of $10^{-4}$, the average contribution of I/O to lookup latency becomes $\approx 1\ \mu s$, which is insignificant compared to other system overheads. The analogous threshold value of $R$ for a flash-based system is $10^{-2}$ since a read I/O to a flash device is in the order of tens to hundreds of microseconds. If there is more main memory in our budget beyond step two, we allocate it to the buffer to further reduce update cost.
sections.translationMarkdown:
现在说明如何在 LSM-tree 设计空间中进行导航。Monkey 通过控制四个调优参数，在查找成本、更新成本和主存占用之间进行精确权衡：合并策略（tiering 或 leveling）、大小比率、分配给过滤器的主存量，以及分配给缓冲区的主存量。我们将说明如何根据数据集（条目的数量和大小）、工作负载（查找与更新的比例）以及存储介质（读写成本比和磁盘块大小）来调节这些参数。我们依据这些参数对最坏情况吞吐量建模，并设计一种算法来找出使吞吐量最大化的调优配置。表 2 列出了新术语。

| 术语 | 定义 | 单位 |
|---|---|---|
| $r$ | 零结果点查找的比例 | |
| $v$ | 非零结果点查找的比例 | |
| $w$ | 更新的比例 | |
| $q$ | 范围查找的比例 | |
| $\hat{M}$ | 在过滤器与缓冲区之间分配的主存 | 位 |
| $\theta$ | 以 I/O 计量的平均操作成本 | I/O |
| $\Omega$ | 从持久存储读取一页的时间 | 秒 |
| $\tau$ | 最坏情况吞吐量 | I/O/秒 |

**表 2：** 用于调优的术语表。

**吞吐量建模。** 首先，对平均操作成本 $\theta$ 建模。公式 7、9、10 和 11 分别给出了零结果点查找成本 $R$、非零结果点查找成本 $V$、范围查找成本 $Q$ 和更新成本 $W$；我们按照它们在工作负载中所占的比例 $r$、$v$、$q$ 和 $w$ 对其加权，注意 $r+v+q+w=1$。结果如公式 12 所示。

$$\theta=r\cdot R+v\cdot V+q\cdot Q+w\cdot W\tag{12}$$

为了得到最坏情况吞吐量 $\tau$，取平均操作成本 $\theta$ 与 $\Omega$ 乘积的倒数，其中 $\Omega$ 是对二级存储执行一次读取所需的时间。结果如公式 13 所示。

$$\tau=1/(\theta\cdot\Omega)\tag{13}$$

**调节大小比率和合并策略。** 合并策略和大小比率是在同一权衡连续体中进行导航的两种互补手段。我们设计了一种分治算法，将这个连续体线性化为一个维度，并找出能使吞吐量最大化的配置。图 10 给出了一个示例，展示该算法为寻找标记为“target”的最优配置而执行的前三次迭代。完整算法见附录 D。

该算法首先将 $T$ 设为 2，并计算这一配置的 $\theta$。随后，它迭代地将空间一分为二，并计算每一部分中心点的 $\theta_1$ 和 $\theta_2$。如果 $\theta_1$ 或 $\theta_2$ 中任一个小于 $\theta$，下一次迭代就将范围缩小至对应的空间分区。否则，算法仍停留在同一点，但探测与当前点距离缩短一倍的点。由于可能的调优配置有 $O(T_{lim})$ 个，且每次迭代都会将问题规模减半，所以该算法的运行时间为 $O(\log_2(T_{lim}))$。在实践中，它只需数毫秒即可运行完毕。

利用该算法，我们还可以对查找成本或更新成本施加上限，以支持不同的服务级别协议（SLA）。为此，算法会丢弃查找成本或更新成本超过所设上限的配置。这相当于在图 10 中为查找成本设置水平界限，或为更新成本设置竖直界限，从而限制搜索空间。

**图 10：** Monkey 使用分治方法搜索设计空间，以找出使吞吐量最大化的调优配置。

**调节主存分配。** 我们提出一种三步策略，在缓冲区和过滤器之间分配可用主存 $\hat{M}$。公式 8 表明，当 $M_{filters}<\frac{M_{threshold}}{T^L}$ 时，过滤器太小，无法产生任何收益。因此，第一步总是将 $\hat{M}$ 的前 $\min(\hat{M},\frac{M_{threshold}}{T^L})$ 位分配给缓冲区。第二步，将剩余主存的 5% 分配给缓冲区，95% 分配给过滤器。该方法近似图 9 中的甜点。我们持续执行第二步，直到假阳性导致的 I/O 开销可以忽略不计，例如与探测 Bloom 过滤器的 CPU 和 RAM 开销相比；我们估计后者处于微秒量级。在基于磁盘的系统中，寻道延迟约为 10 毫秒。这意味着，当公式 7 中假阳性之和 $R$ 达到 $10^{-4}$ 数量级时，I/O 对查找延迟的平均贡献约为 $1\ \mu s$，与其他系统开销相比无足轻重。对于基于闪存的系统，对应的 $R$ 阈值为 $10^{-2}$，因为对闪存设备执行一次读 I/O 需要几十到几百微秒。如果在第二步之后主存预算还有剩余，就将其分配给缓冲区，以进一步降低更新成本。

sections.title: 5. EXPERIMENTAL ANALYSIS
sections.originalMarkdown:
We now proceed to experimentally evaluate Monkey against state-of-the-art designs. We first show that Monkey’s method of tuning the Bloom filters significantly reduces lookup cost across the whole design space and for various workloads. We then demonstrate Monkey’s ability to navigate the design space to find the design that maximizes throughput for a given application workload.

**Experimental Setup.** For experimentation we use a machine with a 500GB 7200RPM disk, 32GB DDR4 main memory, and 4 2.7GHz cores with 8MB L3 cache. The machine is running 64-bit Ubuntu 16.04 LTS, and we run the experiments on an ext4 partition with journaling turned off.

**Implementation.** We implemented Monkey on top of LevelDB, which is a well-known and widely used LSM-tree based key-value store, representative of the state of the art. The current implementation of LevelDB comprises a fixed point in the design space. It only supports leveling as a merge policy, it has a fixed, hard-coded size ratio, and it assigns the same FPR to filters across all levels. We implemented Monkey by adding support for tiering, different size ratios, and optimal FPRs for filters across different levels (by embedding our algorithm from Appendix C). To enable an apples to apples comparison, in all experiments Monkey only differs from LevelDB in how it allocates Bloom filters, and we use the same default values for all other parameters. The default configuration is: size ratio is 2 (the size ratio at which leveling and tiering behave identically); buffer size is 1 MB; the overall amount of main memory allocated to all Bloom filters is $M_{filters}/N = 5$ bits per element (though Monkey allocates these bits differently across different levels). We vary these parameters one at a time to compare across a wide range of the design space.
